title: Offices of Lawyers

labor_share.method: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 541110 ($150,269,970,536) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 541110 ($356,242,120,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

occupations.title: First-Line Supervisors of Office and Administrative Support Workers
Secretaries and Administrative Assistants, Except Legal, Medical, and Executive

n_band.derivation: margin 0.1565 (margins.json[54]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Business & Consumer Services EBITDA/Sales 15.65%); revenue/employee $330,420 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 541110); class <5: 116,668 firms, avg revenue/firm $419,995 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $65,729 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 22,518 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,416,234 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $221,641 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 10,001 firms, avg revenue/firm $3,187,546 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $498,851 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 6,245 firms, avg revenue/firm $10,168,102 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,591,308 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 948 firms, avg revenue/firm $58,651,846 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $9,179,014 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 203 firms, avg revenue/firm $612,665,074 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $95,882,084 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 7,193
n_band.margin_source: Damodaran Jan 2026: Business & Consumer Services EBITDA/Sales 15.65%

ai_replaceable_share.source_fact: No fetched source apportions automatable task fractions by the supplied SOC roles for US NAICS 541110.
ai_replaceable_share.derivation: Judgment by task composition of law-office work; no source establishes these SOC-level fractions.

breakdown_by_role.rationale: Research, drafting, review and first-pass analysis automate materially, while advocacy, negotiation, client counseling, signing and responsibility remain lawyer work.
Document assembly, review, cite checking and docket preparation are highly automatable, with coordination and factual exception work retained.
Formatting, scheduling, intake and filing workflow automate strongly, while client-facing exception handling remains.
General clerical document and data workflow is highly susceptible to automation with supervised exceptions.
Routine inquiry triage and scheduling automate; sensitive client intake and escalation remain human.
Time-entry support, prebill, reconciliation and collections workflow automate, while controls and judgment remain.

breakdown_by_role.role: First-Line Supervisors of Office and Administrative Support Workers
breakdown_by_role.rationale: Workflow scheduling and reporting automate; supervision and employee judgment persist.

breakdown_by_role.role: Secretaries and Administrative Assistants, Except Legal, Medical, and Executive
breakdown_by_role.rationale: Scheduling, correspondence and filing are highly automatable but executive support retains exceptions.
This title-search role is document-intensive and hence automatable, but it is likely a sibling-code artifact rather than representative of law offices.
Billing analytics and reporting automate, while partner management, hiring and strategy remain human judgment.
The residual likely combines law clerks, IT, records, litigation support and marketing with mixed task exposure.

pi_dist.rationale: Mixed fixed fees allow some efficiency retention, but widespread hourly billing and client demand for predictable pricing put much routine-work saving under pass-through pressure.
pi_dist.source_fact: Clio reports that 84% of solos and small firms use hourly rates, while 75% of solos and 65% of small firms use fixed-fee billing models.
pi_dist.derivation: The cited survey establishes a mixed pricing model, not a savings-retention percentage. I assign 0.40 as a conservative judgment: hourly work exposes savings to fewer billed hours, while fixed-fee matters can retain part of the benefit. The page's geography and exact mapping to US NAICS 541110 are not stated, so it is a segment proxy.

citations.title: Legal Trends 2025: Insights for Sole Practitioners & Small Firms
citations.figure_quoted: 75% of solos and 65% of small firms report using fixed fee billing models, while 84% of solos and small firms report using hourly rates.
citations.scope: Clio webpage summarizing solo/small-firm survey findings; page does not state the reporting geography in the quoted passage and is a pricing-segment proxy, not a US NAICS 541110 revenue-weighted measure.

pi_moat.rationale: Professional rules constrain ordinary outside ownership and fee-sharing, but they do not preserve billable hours; clients can substitute routine work or demand lower prices.
pi_moat.source_fact: ABA Model Rule 5.4 says a lawyer or law firm shall not share legal fees with a nonlawyer and shall not form a partnership with a nonlawyer when partnership activities include practicing law.
pi_moat.derivation: The Model Rule evidences a broad professional-structure constraint but is not itself a survey of paid-demand durability or a statute in every jurisdiction. I assign 0.45 to credit a human-lawyer service boundary while discounting for state variation and product-hour substitution.

citations.title: Rule 5.4: Professional Independence of a Lawyer
citations.figure_quoted: A lawyer or law firm shall not share legal fees with a nonlawyer; a lawyer shall not form a partnership with a nonlawyer if partnership activities include practicing law.
citations.scope: ABA Model Rule text; model rule rather than a census of state-law adoption, and it does not measure law-office demand or AI savings capture.

t50_years.source_fact: Clio reports that AI adoption among mid-sized firms reached 93%, with over half using AI widely or universally.
t50_years.derivation: The selected current material-adoption proxy is 0.51, the lowest value consistent with the source's "over half" widely-or-universally condition. Because that is at least 50%, the frozen v3.1 rule sets t50_years to 0. The source's firm-size definition and mapping to US NAICS 541110 are not fully disclosed on this page.
t50_years.plausible_range: 0 years under the selected material-adoption definition; 1-5 years if the relevant target band is materially less adopted than the cited mid-sized sample

citations.title: Clio’s 2025 Legal Trends for Mid-Sized Law Firm Report
citations.figure_quoted: AI adoption among mid-sized firms has skyrocketed to 93%, with over half using AI widely or universally
citations.scope: Clio 2025 report summary for mid-sized law firms; reports adoption among its surveyed legal-professional/firm segment, not a census of all US NAICS 541110 establishments.

current_adoption_pct.source_fact: Clio reports 93% adoption among mid-sized firms and says over half use AI widely or universally.
current_adoption_pct.derivation: The required input is material firm adoption, so I do not use the broader 93% any-use statistic. I set 0.51 as the minimum numerical interpretation of "over half" widely or universally, treating mid-sized firms as an imperfect proxy for the 20-99-employee-heavy target band.

citations.title: Clio’s 2025 Legal Trends for Mid-Sized Law Firm Report
citations.figure_quoted: AI adoption among mid-sized firms has skyrocketed to 93%, with over half using AI widely or universally
citations.scope: Clio 2025 report summary for mid-sized law firms; surveyed segment proxy rather than a US NAICS 541110 establishment census.

historical_analogs.value: Electronic discovery, online research and document-automation diffusion are directional analogs for task compression, but none supplies a transferable firm-level AI adoption or paid-demand curve for law offices.
historical_analogs.source_fact: No fetched source supplies a longitudinal analogue with the required NAICS and material-adoption definition.
historical_analogs.derivation: Qualitative comparison only; no numerical translation is used.

owners_60plus_pct.source_fact: No fetched source reports the age-60-plus share of owners of US NAICS 541110 firms in the defined EBITDA target band.
owners_60plus_pct.derivation: Conservative owner-age judgment; lawyer age distributions cannot be treated as owner distributions without an unfetched bridge.

succession_shortage_documented.evidence: No fetched source directly documents a succession shortage for the specified NAICS and EBITDA band; no formula increment is claimed.

active_consolidators.source_fact: No fetched nationwide source counts active consolidators acquiring US law offices in the specified target band.
active_consolidators.derivation: Judgment reflects sparse traditional law-firm M&A and limited MSO/ABS structures; no source establishes the count.

buy_mult.source_fact: No fetched source establishes normalized EV/EBITDA multiples for $1-10M EBITDA US law-office transactions after market-rate owner compensation.
buy_mult.derivation: Judgment for a practice whose transferable value is discounted for partner portability and owner-comp normalization; no source establishes 4.5x.

exit_mult.source_fact: No fetched source establishes a normalized platform exit EV/EBITDA multiple for US law-office targets after market-rate owner compensation.
exit_mult.derivation: Set equal to buy multiple because ordinary nonlawyer ownership restrictions and rainmaker portability leave no evidenced multiple-arbitrage claim; no source establishes 4.5x.

pricing_structure.value: Law offices use a mixed hourly and fixed-fee model; hourly billing remains widespread while fixed fees are common, with contingency practices forming a materially different, recovery-percentage subsegment.
pricing_structure.source_fact: Clio reports that 84% of solos and small firms use hourly rates, while 75% of solos and 65% of small firms use fixed-fee billing models.
pricing_structure.derivation: The source supports a mixed model rather than NAICS-wide revenue weights. I use it to characterize the target's pricing mechanics, with the caveat that the cited page does not provide a direct US NAICS 541110 revenue breakdown.
pricing_structure.plausible_range: Hourly-dominant mixed model, varying sharply by practice area

citations.title: Legal Trends 2025: Insights for Sole Practitioners & Small Firms
citations.figure_quoted: 75% of solos and 65% of small firms report using fixed fee billing models, while 84% of solos and small firms report using hourly rates.
citations.scope: Clio solo/small-firm survey summary; page-level geography and US NAICS 541110 revenue weights are not stated in the quoted passage.

cross_checks.uplift_vs_A_consistent: The role judgments put automation mainly in paralegal and administrative workflows while retaining a substantial human-lawyer judgment layer. The selected current material-adoption proxy is already at least 50%, so the frozen rule makes A immediate; Python supplies the actual uplift_pp.

terminal_value.justification: AI lowers the cost of producing research, drafts and routine service, but lawyers retain client counseling, advocacy and professional responsibility. The paid product can nevertheless compress because hourly work converts faster production into fewer billable hours and routine consumer/SMB work can be substituted; fixed-fee and contingency matters partially resist that deflation.

cross_checks.no_proxy_confirmed: V relies on task judgments and A only on the adoption evidence and t50 rule. Ownership constraints and any consolidation/multiple estimates are used only in B/M inputs, never as evidence for V or A.
cross_checks.pricing_model_deflation: The mixed pricing evidence maps to pi_dist of 0.40: fixed fees can retain some efficiency, but widespread hourly billing gives clients a route to capture fewer hours. Professional-structure rules support pi_moat of 0.45 only as a partial human-service boundary, not protection for the billable-hour base.

top_uncertain_inputs.plausible_range: 0 years under the selected material-adoption proxy; 1-5 years if the target band is less adopted
top_uncertain_inputs.score_impact: A is 10 at the selected rule boundary but would fall to 10/t50 if target-band material adoption is below 50%.
C moves proportionally with retention and is sensitive to the hourly-versus-fixed-fee revenue mix.
M is highly uncertain because no normalized owner-comp-adjusted target-band transaction comps were fetched.

reviewer_note: Heterogeneous catch-all boundary: score judgment targets conventional law offices, but the code includes plaintiff contingency, insurance defense, corporate, consumer flat-fee and other practice mixes with different deflation and portability. DATASET_MISMATCH: supplied role mix is at four-digit 5411 and includes title examiners, which the prompt identifies as likely 541191 contamination; the injected wage shares and W-2 labor share are used unchanged. Partner profit draws outside W-2 wages may also make labor-share-based V directionally uncertain.

V.arithmetic: Python finalizer: V_raw = labor_share × ai_replaceable_share = 0.4497 × 0.43961 = 0.197692617; V = 10 × min(1, V_raw / 0.25) = 7.90770468

A.arithmetic: Python finalizer: A = 10 when t50_years = 0, otherwise min(10, 10 / t50_years); t50_years = 0, A = 10

M.arithmetic: Python finalizer: M = clamp(4 × (exit_mult − buy_mult) / buy_mult, 0, 10) = 0
